topic: Islam: The Four Rightly Guided Caliphs
subject: religious-studies

items.q: Were the four caliphs prophets like Muhammad?
items.correct: No - they were leaders of the community, not prophets; Muslims believe Muhammad was the final prophet, the 'Seal of the Prophets'.
items.misconception: Each caliph was a new prophet who received revelation.
items.random_wrong: The caliphs were Byzantine governors appointed by the emperor.
items.why: Confusing political succession with prophethood contradicts the core belief in the finality of Muhammad's prophethood.

items.q: Did all Muslims agree about the succession to the Prophet?
items.correct: No - those who became the Shi'a believed Ali had been designated as successor, while others accepted Abu Bakr; this dispute underlies the Sunni-Shi'a division.
items.misconception: The choice of Abu Bakr was accepted unanimously without any disagreement.
items.random_wrong: The succession was decided by the Roman senate.
items.why: The contested succession is the origin of Islam's largest internal division, so smoothing it over misses the topic's key issue.

items.q: Was the era of the four caliphs free of conflict?
items.correct: No - three of the four caliphs were assassinated, and Ali's reign saw the first civil war; 'rightly guided' expresses religious respect, not an absence of strife.
items.misconception: The Rashidun period was a perfectly peaceful golden age.
items.random_wrong: The four caliphs all died peacefully of old age.
items.why: Students read the honorific title as a description of calm; the violent politics of the period is part of the syllabus content.

items.q: Who became the first caliph after the death of the Prophet Muhammad?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Abu Bakr. “Ali” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Which caliph had a standard written text of the Qur'an prepared and distributed?
items.why: Correct answer: Uthman. “Abu Bakr” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: What was Ali's relationship to the Prophet Muhammad?
items.correct: His cousin and son-in-law, married to Fatimah
items.why: The answer examiners want is: His cousin and son-in-law, married to Fatimah. “His brother” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: In what year CE did the Prophet Muhammad die, beginning the era of the caliphs?
items.why: The real figure is 632 CE. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
